chunk_summary: Guidance on upgrading complementary meals for infants 6–24 months to improve energy density, iron, and zinc intake, with practical strategies for nutrient adequacy and developmental needs.
learning_objectives: Understand energy requirements from complementary foods for infants aged 6–23 months.
Learn strategies to enhance energy density and micronutrient content in home-prepared meals.
Identify common feeding errors like energy and micronutrient dilution and how to address them.
Recognize the importance of pairing plant-based iron with vitamin C for better absorption.
key_facts: Energy needs from complementary foods: 200 kcal/day (6–8 months), 300 kcal/day (9–11 months), 550 kcal/day (12–23 months).
Add 1–2 teaspoons of ghee/oil to meals to increase energy density without increasing volume.
Include a protein source in every main meal (e.g., egg, curd, fish, or pulses).
Pair plant-based iron sources with vitamin C (e.g., dal with guava or lemon) to improve absorption.
Limit displacement calories by avoiding sugary drinks, biscuits, and thin rice water.
In vegetarian households, assess risk of vitamin B12 deficiency, especially for developmental health.
reference_quote: For breastfed infants with average milk intake, estimated energy needs from complementary foods are about 200 kcal/day at 6–8 months, 300 kcal/day at 9–11 months, and 550 kcal/day at 12–23 months. — WHO guideline complementary feeding of infants and young children 6–23 months
document_key_topics: Energy and nutrient adequacy in complementary feeding
Strategies to improve iron and zinc intake in infants
Common feeding errors and their solutions
Nutritional risks in vegetarian diets for infants
